name: Filesystem Extended
description: Extended filesystem protection beyond Filesystem Baseline: rm flag variants (-r -f, --recursive --force), find -delete, shred, truncate, tar --remove-files, dd file overwrites, unlink, and copy-then-delete exfiltration patterns on sensitive paths. Derived from the destructive_command_guard pattern library.

rules.name: Require access request for cp sensitive then delete
rules.description: archive copy of a sensitive path into temp followed by forced recursive deletion is a cross-segment data-loss bypass. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the sensitive source path before propagating it.

rules.name: Require access request for ln symlink sensitive then delete
rules.description: symlink from a sensitive path into temp followed by forced recursive deletion can traverse and destroy the target. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the sensitive source path before propagating it.

rules.name: Require access request for rsync sensitive then delete
rules.description: rsync archive of a sensitive path into temp followed by forced recursive deletion is a cross-segment data-loss bypass. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the sensitive source path before propagating it.

rules.name: Require access request for rm rf root home
rules.description: rm -rf on root or home paths is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. This command will NOT be executed. Ask the user to run it manually if truly needed. Safer alternative: `find {path} -type f | head -20` — Preview what files would be deleted before running.

rules.name: Require access request for rm r f separate root home
rules.description: rm with separate -r -f flags targeting root or home is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `find {path} -type f | head -20` — Preview what files would be deleted before running.

rules.name: Require access request for rm recursive force root home
rules.description: rm --recursive --force targeting root or home is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `find {path} -type f | head -20` — Preview what files would be deleted before running.

rules.name: Require access request for find delete root home
rules.description: find <sensitive-path> -delete is bytewise-equivalent to rm -rf on root/home and is EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. This command will NOT be executed. Safer alternative: `find {path} -type f | head -20` — Preview which files `-delete` would remove (drop the -delete flag).

rules.name: Require access request for unlink root home
rules.description: unlink on a sensitive system or home path is one-shot data destruction with no recovery. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the path before unlinking.

rules.name: Require access request for truncate zero root home
rules.description: truncate -s 0|-N on a sensitive system or home path destroys data. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `cp {path} {path}.bak && truncate -s 0 {path}` — Make a backup before zeroing the file.

rules.name: Require access request for shred root home
rules.description: shred on a sensitive system or home path destroys data beyond forensic recovery. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the path before shredding (no recovery).

rules.name: Require justification for rm rf general
rules.description: rm -rf is destructive and requires human approval. Explain what you want to delete and why, then ask the user to run the command manually. Safer alternative: `rm -ri {path}` — Interactive mode: confirms each file before deletion.

rules.name: Require access request for tar remove files root home
rules.description: tar --remove-files on a sensitive system or home path is recursive deletion masquerading as an archive operation. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `tar -cf {path}.tar {path}` — Archive without --remove-files (sources are preserved).

rules.name: Require justification for rm r f separate
rules.description: rm with separate -r -f flags is destructive and requires human approval. Safer alternative: `rm -ri {path}` — Interactive mode: confirms each file before deletion.

rules.name: Require access request for dd overwrite root home
rules.description: dd of=<sensitive-path> overwrites file contents in place. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS on a system or home file. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the path before overwriting (no recovery).

rules.name: Require justification for rm recursive force long
rules.description: rm --recursive --force is destructive and requires human approval. Safer alternative: `rm --interactive --recursive {path}` — Interactive mode: confirms each file before deletion.

rules.name: Require access request for mv sensitive source root home
rules.description: mv touching a sensitive system or home path is the cross-segment recursive-force-delete bypass. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the source path before any move.

rules.name: Require justification for find delete general
rules.description: find ... -delete is destructive (bytewise-equivalent to rm -rf on the matched tree) and requires human approval. Safer alternative: `find {path} -type f | head -20` — Preview which files `-delete` would remove (drop the -delete flag).

rules.name: Require access request for redirect truncate root home
rules.description: shell redirect (>, >|, &>, >&, 1>, 2>) to a sensitive system or home path truncates the file to zero bytes. EXTREMELY DANGEROUS. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the path before any redirect.

rules.name: Require justification for unlink general
rules.description: unlink is destructive (POSIX equivalent of rm on a single file) and requires human approval. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the path before unlinking.

rules.name: Require justification for truncate zero general
rules.description: truncate -s 0|-N is destructive (zeroes or shrinks file content) and requires human approval. Safer alternative: `cp {path} {path}.bak && truncate -s 0 {path}` — Make a backup before zeroing the file.

rules.name: Require justification for shred general
rules.description: shred destroys file content beyond recovery and requires human approval. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the path before shredding (no recovery).

rules.name: Require justification for tar remove files general
rules.description: tar --remove-files deletes source paths after archiving and requires human approval. Safer alternative: `tar -cf {path}.tar {path}` — Archive without --remove-files (sources are preserved).

rules.name: Require justification for dd overwrite general
rules.description: dd with of=<file> overwrites file contents and requires human approval. Safer alternative: `ls -la {path}` — Verify the path before overwriting (no recovery).
